basics.title: Open and restore
paragraphs: Put a book file into your Obsidian vault and click it to open. The reader automatically saves and restores reading progress, typography preferences, and the current reading state.
Currently supported formats are .epub, .mobi, .azw3, .fb2, .fbz, .txt, and .cbz. In practice, EPUB, FB2/FBZ, and TXT are better for structured export, while CBZ is more image-oriented.

basics.title: Sidebar and bookshelf
paragraphs: The reader supports both a local sidebar and a global bookshelf view so you can switch between books, the table of contents, and annotations inside the same note-taking workspace.

items: TOC: jump quickly by chapter.
Notes: review synced highlights, thoughts, and related content for the current book.
Bookmarks: manage bookmarks for the current page.
My Bookshelf: browse book files in the vault and return from the bookshelf subview to the current book sidebar.

listGroups.heading: Reading flow
items: Paged mode: best for linear reading and keyboard page turns.
Scrolled mode: best for longer passages, footnote round-trips, and quickly searching nearby context.

listGroups.heading: Page layout
items: Single page: the default layout for most situations.
Double column: closer to the feel of an open book and automatically switches to a wider display.

listGroups.heading: Width mode
items: standard: standard reading width for long-form immersive reading.
full: a wider layout that still keeps comfortable margins on larger screens.
edge: a true edge-to-edge mode that uses as much host width as possible.

basics.title: Display settings and helpers
paragraphs: Reader settings cover more than width. They also include typography, footnotes, and top information display so you can tune the experience by book type.

items: The typography panel adjusts line height, letter spacing, and page margins, and can restore defaults.
Scrolled mode can independently toggle the right-side page buttons.
Footnote marker clicks can switch between showing a footnote popover and jumping directly to the footnote.

highlight.title: Annotation styles
paragraphs: Selecting text opens the toolbar and supports color highlights plus multiple inline annotation styles. Color, style, and location data are all saved and restored together.

listGroups.heading: Styles
items: Highlight: best for standard excerpts.
Underline: keeps the page background while emphasizing key sentences.
Strikethrough: useful for content to ignore, revise, or conceal.
Wavy underline: useful for problems, uncertainties, or passages that need another pass.

highlight.title: Thought bubbles and footnote popovers
paragraphs: Thought bubbles in the text are additional cues. They do not replace or weaken the highlight, underline, strikethrough, or wavy underline itself.
As long as the excerpt contains a `---div---` separator, the reader shows a thought bubble beside the highlight. You can edit it from the bubble or from the toolbar thought action.
Footnote marker clicks can be configured to show a popover preview or jump directly to the footnote location.

items: Highlights, styles, and thoughts automatically reappear when you reopen the same book.
Data sources consistently support Markdown, Canvas, and card data instead of relying on a separate black-box database.
Source links are the authoritative record for restoring and locating excerpts. As long as the link remains valid, you can freely rewrite the excerpt body.
When a note file changes, the reader reloads the related highlights so the book view and note view stay in sync.

workflow.title: How automated excerpts work
paragraphs: In the reader, selecting text and highlighting only shows briefly — it does not save excerpt data. To keep excerpts permanently, create or choose a Markdown note, Canvas, or Weave destination, pick where to insert, and the reader will write to that location automatically.
Many users expect highlighting alone to save a note. In practice, you must create or choose a target file first; only then is the excerpt written to your vault for long-term storage.

listGroups.heading: Saving and aggregating excerpts
items: Reading excerpts live in Markdown, Canvas, and Weave cards; the reader aggregates them in one place.
You can edit card body text freely, but do not change source links — that breaks location and re-display.
To save permanently, use the selection toolbar to pick a target: create or choose Markdown, bind Canvas, or excerpt to Weave.

workflow.title: Auto mode and the selection toolbar
paragraphs: Auto mode decides the default output path after selecting text: off leans toward copying, while on leans toward writing directly into your current workflow.

items: Auto mode off: copy to the clipboard for temporary quoting.
Auto mode on: better for writing directly into the current workflow and also participates in Canvas-linked flows.
The selection toolbar supports copy or insert, search, card creation, reading points, AI actions, and more.

workflow.title: Excerpt format and source links
paragraphs: The reader tries to attach precise location data to excerpts so you can jump back from notes to the source passage.
If an excerpt includes a thought, putting it after the `---div---` separator is recommended so the thought bubble can appear in the text automatically.

items: Current-page bookmark: leave a quick return point for the current page.
Last reading point: save the recent reading location you want to keep long term.
Reading reference point: record a main reading anchor so you can return after branching out.
Incremental reading resume point: work with incremental reading as the next entry point for processing.

tools.title: Export to Markdown
paragraphs: Use the reader view menu as the export entry point when you want to turn reading output into long-term vault notes.

items: Export current chapter: convert the current chapter to Markdown and keep images as Obsidian-friendly attachments whenever possible.
Export book highlights: organize annotations and excerpts from the current book for later refinement.

listGroups.heading: Screenshots
items: In screenshot mode you can drag a capture area over the reading viewport.
Image mode: save as an image file and insert directly when auto mode is on.
Embed mode: extract text when possible and generate a location-aware reference; when auto mode is off it prefers copying the result.

listGroups.heading: Canvas
items: You can create a new Canvas map for the current book or bind an existing Canvas.
After binding, you can open it, keep editing it, or disconnect it, which works well for chapter maps, question trees, and reading routes.

shortcuts.description: When paged mode is active and the EPUB view is focused, move to the previous or next page (scrolled mode does not use arrow keys).
In paragraph reading mode, move within or across paragraphs when the paragraph panel is focused.
Close the tutorial, or dismiss the currently focused reader overlay.

buttons.label: Sidebar
buttons.description: Switch between TOC, notes, bookmarks, and bookshelf-related entry points.

buttons.label: Typography
buttons.description: Adjust line height, letter spacing, page margins, and other reading parameters.

buttons.label: Reading flow
buttons.description: Switch between paged and scrolled reading.

buttons.label: Page layout
buttons.description: Switch between single-page and double-column layouts.

buttons.label: Reference point
buttons.description: Save or update the reading reference anchor.

buttons.label: Canvas map
buttons.description: Create, bind, or manage the reading map.

buttons.label: Auto mode
buttons.description: Switch between copy-first and auto-insert output.

family.title: What is Weave
paragraphs: Weave is a family of Obsidian plugins built around long-term learning: read → capture → schedule → review.

Weave EPUB Reader (this plugin) handles in-vault reading and excerpts; the Weave main plugin handles memory decks, question banks, and smart card creation; Weave Incremental Reading handles reading-point queues and calendar scheduling.

listGroups.heading: How the three plugins divide work
items: Weave EPUB Reader: read books in Obsidian, manage highlights and excerpts, and connect with notes, Canvas, and decks.
Weave main plugin: create memory cards with source links from any paragraph in any vault file, and manage and review them in table, kanban, and grid views.
Weave Incremental Reading: organize “read later” material into reading-point queues and advance multiple books and chapters through topics and a month calendar.

family.title: Weave EPUB Reader (this plugin)
paragraphs: Make Obsidian more than a note archive—a place where you actually read: open EPUB, TXT, and other books in your vault, excerpt as you go, and keep passages linked to your notes through deep links and in-body rendering.

listGroups.heading: Core capabilities
items: Bookshelf and reading: multi-format library, TOC navigation, paged/scrolled modes, typography and themes, progress and bookmarks.
Excerpts and highlights: selection toolbar, five highlight colors and linear mark styles, thought bubbles, auto or manual capture to Markdown, Canvas, or Weave decks.
Tracing and re-display: excerpts keep location data; highlights refresh when notes change; jump from a highlight back to the source note, Canvas, or deck.
Extended workflows: chapter export, screenshots, Canvas binding, paragraph reading mode; card creation and AI via the Weave main plugin; chapter scheduling and reading points via incremental reading.
And more

family.title: Weave main plugin
paragraphs: Weave is an Obsidian-based knowledge card system: excerpt any paragraph from any file in your vault to create memory cards with source-tracing links. Every card can keep location metadata so you can jump back to the original passage.
Browse and manage all Weave cards and decks in table view, kanban view, and grid card view.

listGroups.heading: Core capabilities
items: Excerpt to card: create memory cards from paragraphs or selections in Markdown, PDF, the EPUB reader, Canvas, and other sources, each with a link back to the source.
Multi-view management: browse, filter, and organize all cards and decks in table, kanban, and grid card views.

Sidebar grid view: dock the grid card view in Obsidian’s sidebar to filter cards by the currently active Markdown file; also supports AI card creation from the active document, parsing and importing cards.
Question bank and practice: parse question-bank Markdown files, import questions, and start exercise tests; includes error book and exam statistics.
Spaced repetition: FSRS scheduling, study sessions, progress stats, and deck management.
And more

family.title: Weave Incremental Reading
paragraphs: For when you save a lot but never read deeply across many books: turn scattered material from Markdown, PDF selections, Canvas, web pages, and EPUB chapters into a schedulable, resumable, source-linked reading-point queue—advanced through a calendar instead of willpower alone.

listGroups.heading: Core capabilities
items: Reading points and topics (IRDeck): create points from document selections, block links, reader selections, and more; organize them into .irdeck topics.
Incremental reading calendar: see pending, scheduled, and overdue points by date and plan daily reading tasks.
Scheduling and resume: daily limits, time budgets, interleaved reading, and one-click return to the exact source position.
Source jump-back: reading points keep source metadata so you can return to Markdown, PDF, EPUB, and other origins.
Reader integration: add chapters from this reader’s TOC to incremental reading; create reading points with EPUB source deep links from selected text.
And more

family.title: How to combine them
paragraphs: The three plugins have separate roles and can be mixed as needed. Common setups:

items: Read and excerpt only in Obsidian: Weave EPUB Reader alone is enough.
Review and make cards after reading: EPUB Reader + Weave main plugin—fill the card window from excerpts or use AI split-to-card.
Pace multiple or long books: EPUB Reader + Weave Incremental Reading—schedule chapters on the calendar and deep-link back on due days.
Full loop (read → capture → schedule → review): install all three—excerpt while reading, review with cards, queue chapters and passages for incremental reading.

credits.title: EPUB parsing and reading engine
paragraphs: EPUB reading is now fully based on the foliate-js engine, while historical reading positions and existing data are migrated as compatibly as possible.

credits.title: Links and knowledge capture
paragraphs: The precise EPUB link and jump-back workflow was inspired by the excellent ideas behind Obsidian PDF++ and combined with Obsidian backlinks and graph capabilities.

credits.title: Reading and Canvas integration
paragraphs: The integration between the EPUB reader and Obsidian Canvas was inspired by MarginNote-style reading and mind-map workflows while continuing to evolve within Obsidian’s native file system.

credits.title: Paragraph mode inspiration
paragraphs: The paragraph reading mode was inspired by the paragraph mode experience in the Readest reading app, then adapted to fit this reader’s location, excerpt, and footnote workflows.

credits.title: Toolbar interaction reference
paragraphs: The text-selection toolbar interaction takes inspiration from the reading toolbar experience in WeRead.

credits.title: Dictionary plugin compatibility
paragraphs: Thanks to the community-built Obsidian plugin `obsdian-fingertip-translation`. It originally brought in-editor word-lookup support to Obsidian Markdown editing and can now also work inside this EPUB reader after compatibility handling.
The reader itself does not bundle a dictionary. Dictionary explanations and related popovers are provided by that external plugin.
